From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Study of convergence properties of statistical estimators

In [statistics](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics"), **asymptotic theory**, or **large sample theory**, is a framework for assessing properties of [estimators](/wiki/Estimator "Estimator") and [statistical tests](/wiki/Statistical_test "Statistical test"). Within this framework, it is often assumed that the [sample size](/wiki/Sample_size "Sample size") *n* may grow indefinitely; the properties of estimators and tests are then evaluated under the limit of *n* → ∞. In practice, a limit evaluation is considered to be approximately valid for large finite sample sizes too.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asymptotic_theory_(statistics)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

Most statistical problems begin with a dataset of [size](/wiki/Sample_size "Sample size") *n*. The asymptotic theory proceeds by assuming that it is possible (in principle) to keep collecting additional data, thus that the sample size grows infinitely, i.e. *n* → ∞. Under the assumption, many results can be obtained that are unavailable for samples of finite size. An example is the [weak law of large numbers](/wiki/Law_of_large_numbers "Law of large numbers"). The law states that for a sequence of [independent and identically distributed](/wiki/Independent_and_identically_distributed_random_variables "Independent and identically distributed random variables") (IID) [random variables](/wiki/Random_variable "Random variable") *X*1, *X*2, ..., if one value is drawn from each random variable and the average of the first *n* values is computed as *X**n*, then the *X**n* [converge in probability](/wiki/Convergence_of_random_variables#Convergence_in_probability "Convergence of random variables") to the population mean E[*Xi*] as *n* → ∞.[[2]](#cite_note-ONE-2)

In asymptotic theory, the standard approach is *n* → ∞. For some [statistical models](/wiki/Statistical_model "Statistical model"), slightly different approaches of asymptotics may be used. For example, with [panel data](/wiki/Panel_data "Panel data"), it is commonly assumed that one dimension in the data remains fixed, whereas the other dimension grows: *T* = constant and *N* → ∞, or vice versa.[[2]](#cite_note-ONE-2)

Besides the standard approach to asymptotics, other alternative approaches exist:

* Within the [local asymptotic normality](/wiki/Local_asymptotic_normality "Local asymptotic normality") framework, it is assumed that the value of the "true parameter" in the model varies slightly with *n*, such that the *n*-th model corresponds to  *θn* = *θ* + *h*/√*n* . This approach lets us study the [regularity of estimators](/wiki/Regular_estimator "Regular estimator").
* When [statistical tests](/wiki/Statistical_test "Statistical test") are studied for their power to distinguish against the alternatives that are close to the null hypothesis, it is done within the so-called "local alternatives" framework: the null hypothesis is *H*0: *θ* = *θ*0 and the alternative is *H*1: *θ* = *θ*0 + *h*/√*n* . This approach is especially popular for the [unit root tests](/wiki/Unit_root_test "Unit root test").
* There are models where the dimension of the parameter space Θ*n* slowly expands with *n*, reflecting the fact that the more observations there are, the more structural effects can be feasibly incorporated in the model.
* In [kernel density estimation](/wiki/Kernel_density_estimation "Kernel density estimation") and [kernel regression](/wiki/Kernel_regression "Kernel regression"), an additional parameter is assumed—the bandwidth *h*. In those models, it is typically taken that *h* → 0 as *n* → ∞. The rate of convergence must be chosen carefully, though, usually *h* ∝ *n*−1/5.

In many cases, highly accurate results for finite samples can be obtained via numerical methods (i.e. computers); even in such cases, though, asymptotic analysis can be useful. This point was made by [Small (2010](#CITEREFSmall2010), §1.4), as follows.

> A primary goal of asymptotic analysis is to obtain a deeper *qualitative* understanding of *quantitative* tools. The conclusions of an asymptotic analysis often supplement the conclusions which can be obtained by numerical methods.

## Modes of convergence of random variables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asymptotic_theory_(statistics)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Modes of convergence of random variables")]

Further information: [Convergence of random variables](/wiki/Convergence_of_random_variables "Convergence of random variables")

## Asymptotic properties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asymptotic_theory_(statistics)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Asymptotic properties")]

### Estimators

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asymptotic_theory_(statistics)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Estimators")]

#### *[Consistency](/wiki/Consistent_estimator "Consistent estimator")*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asymptotic_theory_(statistics)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Consistency")]

A sequence of estimates is said to be *consistent*, if it [converges in probability](/wiki/Converges_in_probability "Converges in probability") to the true value of the parameter being estimated:

:   ![{\displaystyle {\hat {\theta }}_{n}\ \xrightarrow {\overset {}{p}} \ \theta _{0}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bda7929649f6f9b274bf52e17bc4ca38ec19780a)

That is, roughly speaking with an infinite amount of data the [estimator](/wiki/Estimator "Estimator") (the formula for generating the estimates) would almost surely give the correct result for the parameter being estimated.[[2]](#cite_note-ONE-2)

#### *[Asymptotic distribution](/wiki/Asymptotic_distribution "Asymptotic distribution")*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asymptotic_theory_(statistics)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Asymptotic distribution")]

If it is possible to find sequences of non-random constants {*an*}, {*bn*} (possibly depending on the value of *θ*0), and a non-degenerate distribution *G* such that

:   ![{\displaystyle b_{n}({\hat {\theta }}_{n}-a_{n})\ \xrightarrow {d} \ G,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6d1ec2b2ffb5c947ffbe100c64a6426ff4e7aaec)

then the sequence of estimators ![{\displaystyle \textstyle {\hat {\theta }}_{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c6e124ed843b0309757fcef457927f5cb269d400) is said to have the *[asymptotic distribution](/wiki/Asymptotic_distribution "Asymptotic distribution")* *G*.

Most often, the estimators encountered in practice are [asymptotically normal](/wiki/Estimator#Asymptotic_normality "Estimator"), meaning their asymptotic distribution is the [normal distribution](/wiki/Normal_distribution "Normal distribution"), with *an* = *θ*0, *bn* = √*n*, and *G* = [*N*(0, *V*)](/wiki/Normal_distribution "Normal distribution"):

:   ![{\displaystyle {\sqrt {n}}({\hat {\theta }}_{n}-\theta _{0})\ \xrightarrow {d} \ {\mathcal {N}}(0,V).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/898c34e4e09d807fe30938c22e3e0c306f7e2cb0)

#### *Asymptotic [confidence regions](/wiki/Confidence_region "Confidence region")*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asymptotic_theory_(statistics)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Asymptotic confidence regions")]
